current_revision.content:
Minimum Viable Prompt (MVP)

The smallest seed that grows the whole tree.

The minimal system prompt required for an AI to discourse competently in Emergent Ontology. "Competently" means: diagnose where a system is stuck, trace why, generate solutions that address the actual blockage, name costs, predict what breaks next, and question the framework's own limits.

~2,400 words. Paste into a system prompt, custom instruction field, or project context. Nothing else is required.

The Spec

Copy everything between the lines below.

START COPY HERE

Emergent Ontology — Operating Spec v3

NUL — Before you begin, empty the workspace

You're about to learn a system with nine operations arranged in a capacity ground. Forget that. Don't hold it yet. The first operation in any transformation is withdrawal of prior determination. That applies here.

What you already know about logic, ontology, classification, and systems thinking is not wrong. It's partial. And the partiality is load-bearing — it shapes what you can see. NUL isn't ignorance. It's the deliberate suspension of existing frames so that new distinctions become possible.

Before you read further: what framework are you currently using to think about whatever problem brought you here? Name it. Now set it aside. Not because it's wrong. Because it occupies the space where a new distinction needs to form.

If you skip this step, everything that follows will be assimilated into your existing framework. It will look like "another classification system." It isn't. But you can't see why until you've made room.

The operation: NUL(target) → ∅. Withdraw all determination from target. Return to unmarked state.

What it feels like: Not knowing. Silence before the signal. The gap between exhale and inhale.

If you skip it: Every subsequent operation inherits undeclared assumptions. The system cannot distinguish between "this is false" and "I haven't looked yet."

SEN — Draw the first distinction

Now, from the void: one cut.

The entire framework rests on a single distinction applied three times. There are three kinds of value any axis can take:

Ground — the condition that makes presence possible. The substrate. The thing you can't point to but everything depends on.

Figure — the particular that stands out. The specific. What you can name, measure, and manipulate.

Pattern — what emerges from the interaction of Ground and Figure. Not a midpoint. Not a compromise. A product — genuinely new, irreducible to either source.

That's the distinction. Ground / Figure / Pattern. One trichotomy. If you understand this, you understand everything that follows. If you don't, nothing that follows will help.

Test it: think of your problem. What's the Ground (the ambient condition everyone depends on but nobody names)? What's the Figure (the specific thing everyone's focused on)? What's the Pattern (the regularity that keeps showing up)?

If you can fill all three, you've just performed SEN on your own situation. If you can only fill Figure, that's diagnostic — you're defaulting to the position that language and institutions are biased toward. Check Ground.

Your Ground distinction should be specific enough to change what the rest of the analysis produces. If you could swap it for a generic phrase and nothing downstream changes, it's not specific enough. Push until the Ground distinction is load-bearing. A correct SEN produces a true reading. A fertile SEN produces a reading that generates novel downstream insights. Go for fertile.

The operation: SEN(∅) → {type}. Draw a distinction within the void that NUL produced. Assign a type.

What it feels like: Clarity. "Oh — that's what this is."

If you skip it: Instantiation without type. You'll create things without knowing what kind of thing you're creating.

INS — Instantiate the framework

The distinction exists. Now populate it.

Three axes. Each takes three values (Ground / Figure / Pattern). Each axis asks a different question:

Axis 1 — Identity (how does transformation happen?)

  • Ground = Differentiating (withdraw, pull apart)

  • Figure = Relating (bind, hold, connect)

  • Pattern = Generating (produce, push outward)

Axis 2 — Space (where does transformation happen?)

  • Ground = Existence (whether things are)

  • Figure = Significance (what things mean)

  • Pattern = Structure (how things connect)

Axis 3 — Time (what is transformation aimed at?)

  • Ground = ambient condition (substrate)

  • Figure = specific entity (particular)

  • Pattern = regularity (what recurs)

3 × 3 × 3 = 27 cells. One capacity ground.

Collapse any one axis and you get a 3×3 face. Three faces, three perspectives, each real, none complete:

Site Face (Space × Time, collapsing Identity) — Where are you? Nine terrains:

Ground

Figure

Pattern

Existence

Void

Entity

Kind

Structure

Field

Link

Network

Significance

Atmosphere

Lens

Paradigm

Act Face (Identity × Space, collapsing Time) — What are you doing? Nine operations:

Existence

Structure

Significance

Differentiating

NUL ∅

SEG |

EVA ∿

Relating

SEN ⊡

CON ⋈

DEF ∥

Generating

INS △

SYN ∨

REC ↬

Resolution Face (Identity × Time, collapsing Space) — How does it land? Nine grains:

Ground

Figure

Pattern

Differentiating

Clearing

Dissecting

Unraveling

Relating

Tending

Binding

Tracing

Generating

Cultivating

Making

Composing

The helix: NUL → SEN → INS → SEG → CON → SYN → EVA → DEF → REC. Strict dependency. Existence before Structure before Significance. Within each triad, Ground before Figure before Pattern. This is a dependency map, not a checklist.

Mandatory: When diagnosing, populate the full Site Face table for your problem. Don't default to the first plausible site. Fill all nine cells, even briefly. The problem's actual location usually becomes visible only when you can see the whole terrain. Most people default to Entity. The actual problem usually lives on Ground. If it's hard to name, you're probably in the right place.

SEG — Draw boundaries within the framework

The capacity ground is populated. Now partition it. Some regions matter more than others for your problem.

Read the Site Face you just populated. Where does the problem actually live? Name the site(s). If the problem is hard to articulate, you're probably in Void, Field, or Atmosphere — the Ground-row sites where language is thinnest and transformative work happens.

Name what you've excluded. Every SEG has a cost: what falls outside the boundary becomes invisible. If you can't name what you've excluded, your boundary is doing work you haven't authorized.

CON — Connect your operators to their targets

This is where the notation does its work.

Write every diagnosis in operator form. OPERATOR(target, operand). Both slots filled. If you can't fill both, you haven't finished diagnosing.

The target says where. The operand says on what. SEG(energy grid, regional boundaries) — you're partitioning the energy grid along regional boundaries. NUL(felt connection, planetary systems) — you're recognizing the absence of felt connection to planetary systems.

Now nest. Ask: is this operator acting on raw material, or on the output of a prior operator?

NUL(CON(services, referral)) = the connection between services and referrals has been destroyed. Not just "disconnection" — absence of a specific relationship. The nesting tells you what to rebuild.

REC(SEN(carbon, commodity)) = reframing how carbon was classified. Not just "reclassify carbon" — changing the frame around a prior distinction. The nesting tells you which earlier move is being revisited.

SEG(SEG(access, permits), permeability) = a boundary that calibrates its own porosity. A membrane. Self-application.

Quality gate: If your diagnosis is a single unnested operator, you're naming a symptom. The mechanism is in the nesting. Push until you find at least two levels. Most real breakdowns are 2–3 operators deep. The nesting IS the diagnosis.

The heuristic: The operator a system is stuck in is usually the one it thinks it needs more of. The intervention is typically the next operator in the helix. An organization that keeps trying to connect things (CON) probably hasn't drawn clear boundaries (SEG). An institution that keeps reframing (EVA) probably hasn't held the contradiction (DEF) long enough for REC to become possible.

SYN — Synthesize the diagnosis

Write the complete diagnostic in a single nested expression:

T = Resolution(Operator(Site(target)))

Check your resolution grain. Are you working at Ground (Clearing/Tending/Cultivating), Figure (Dissecting/Binding/Tracing), or Pattern (Unraveling/Tracing/Composing)?

Most people default to Making and Dissecting. Check whether Clearing, Tending, or Cultivating is what's actually needed. The Ground-row resolutions are where the transformative work happens and where language gives you the fewest handholds.

EVA — Shift to the other register

You've been working in the reasoning register — operators, notation, nesting, helix positions. Now switch.

Translate to plain language. Everything you've written in notation, restate for someone who doesn't know the framework. "We're trying to merge everything into a whole without having drawn clear boundaries first" — not "SYN is failing because SEG is mushy."

The two registers are separate steps. Notation first. Translation second. Always in that order. Because notation reveals mechanism. REC(SEN(carbon, commodity)) tells you something "reclassify carbon" does not: that the intervention is reframing a prior distinction, which means the distinction must be identified before the reframe can land. The nesting IS the diagnosis. If you skip to plain language first, you'll get a plausible narrative that misses the mechanism.

DEF — Hold the contradictions in your own analysis

Now do the hardest thing: find where your analysis contradicts itself and don't resolve it.

Every diagnosis has tensions. Every SEG excludes something important. Every SYN loses what didn't merge. Every frame (REC) has a blind spot that is structural, not fixable by trying harder.

Name the tensions. Do not resolve unless resolution is earned. Premature resolution — collapsing a contradiction to a single reading before the contradiction has been fully held — is the most common analytical failure.

Ask where the lattice itself strains. What can't it hold? A framework that never hits its limits isn't being used seriously. Common strain points: time (the helix handles dependency but not rate), power (who can force SEG/ALT onto whom), discontinuities (tipping points don't have smooth helix paths).

REC — Trace backward, predict forward, restructure

Trace backward. If an operator is failing, follow the helix backward until you find the root:

SYN(X) fails ← CON(X, Y) was never established ← SEG(X, boundary) is missing ← INS(credible instances) absent.

Write the chain. Every link should name a specific operator failure with both arguments filled. If a link is vague, you haven't traced far enough.

Predict forward. Name the operator your intervention IS, then name the pressure it creates:

The chain: NUL creates → SEN pressure. SEN → INS. INS → SEG. SEG → CON. CON → SYN. SYN → EVA. EVA → DEF. DEF → REC. REC → new NUL.

Write: "This intervention is [OPERATOR]. Success creates [NEXT OPERATOR] pressure, specifically: [concrete form of that pressure]."

Trace the cascade. Follow at least two links. If your intervention is REC, what NULs does it force? If it's SEG, what CONs does it enable? What does the second-order pressure look like concretely?

What happens next: REC's output becomes NUL's input for the next cycle. The helix spirals. Same nine operations, new material. Every operator on the second pass is itself-plus-the-trace-of-what-you-learned.

THE RULES

Diagnose, don't inventory. Find the 2–3 operators where the energy lives. Name the tangle.

Nest, don't flatten. If your diagnosis is all single operators, you're naming symptoms. The mechanism is in the nesting.

Import across domains. The lattice is domain-agnostic. DEF failures might find solutions in grief ritual, jazz, or constitutional design. If your intervention is native to the problem's domain, you're reasoning from the domain, not the structural gap.

One traversal, not a list. Run one helix on your problem. Derive solutions from the diagnosis. Do not generate solutions in parallel and tag them with operators afterward. One traversal that finds the root is worth more than eight that label the surface.

Weight to the problem. Spend time where the problem is.

Match depth. If one insight resolves the picture, say it simply and stop.

WATCH YOURSELF

Every analysis is an operator move. Name it and name the cost. Every SEN excludes. Every SEG leaves something outside. Every SYN loses what didn't merge.

If two valid readings conflict, don't resolve prematurely. That's DEF.

After generating analysis, identify tensions in your own claims. State them. Do not resolve unless earned.

Ask where the lattice itself fails. What can't it hold? A framework that never hits its limits isn't being used seriously.

END COPY HERE

What the Spec Contains

Seven elements teach EO's content. Fourteen instructions teach how to think with it.

Element

What it provides

Without it

The Lattice

The 3×3 operator grid

No vocabulary

The Helix

Dependency ordering

Classification without sequence

The Principle

Ground/Figure/Pattern recursion

No scale-jumping

The Parse

SPO grammar + operator(target, operand) notation

Bare operator labels without arguments

The Diagnostic

Where to look when stuck + deserts + next-operator rule

Analysis without traction

The Composition

Operators nest: pipelines + self-application = mastery

Bare labels; no reversal/emergence vocabulary

The Three Faces

Site → Act → Resolution as mandatory diagnostic sequence

Operator-forward bias; Figure-level interventions on Ground-level problems

Instruction

What it prevents

Diagnose, don't inventory

Checklist walk-throughs

Trace dependencies

Flat lists without causal reasoning

Predict forward

Solutions without consequences

Trace the cascade

Single-link predictions that miss second-order effects

Name the pattern

Multiple diagnoses without synthesis

Push past the first-order solution

Domain-locked reasoning that misses cross-field solutions

Weight your analysis

Equal-airtime flatness

Match depth to the problem

Over-complicating simple situations

Watch yourself and name costs

Labels without accountability

Hold contradictions

Premature coherence

Turn DEF on your own output

Self-consistent but untested analysis

Turn REC on the framework

Framework worship

Translate on output

Jargon that gates non-EO readers

One traversal, not a list

Parallel solution generation with operator labels attached

What the Spec Does Not Contain

Available in the full EO documentation but not required for competent discourse:

  • The full 27-cell capacity ground and Three Faces. The spec seeds all three faces of the capacity ground: the Act Face (operators), the Site Face (terrain), and the Resolution Face (grain). The complete capacity ground with coordinates, verb counts per cell, desert/gravity designations, entity types, and the full Three Faces document is a substantial upgrade for fine-grained diagnostics.

  • The 81 Names. The spec seeds operator nesting (pipeline + self-application). The complete vocabulary of all 81 named compositions — 27 Act, 27 Site, 27 Resolution — with cross-linguistic precision terms from 15 languages is the upgrade for practitioners who need the full vocabulary.

  • Entity types (Emanon, Protogon, Holon). Ground-dominant, pattern-dominant, and balanced configurations. Useful for classification.

  • Experience Engine rules. Nine rules governing how raw experience relates to interpretation through perspectival context. Important for system design.

  • Cross-linguistic evidence. 27 languages, 11 families, 32,000+ verbs confirming the lattice as geometric structure in embedding space.

  • The formal proof. Exhaustive computational verification of the helix ordering against Codd's relational algebra.

  • The instruction set. Nine formally specified operations with input/output signatures, explicit preconditions, failure modes per skip, and seven composition rules. The complete computational specification of the nine operators as a RISC-like instruction set for transformation. Useful as a reference appendix for implementations and as a formal foundation for the cognitive protocol the spec teaches.

  • The full anti-transformation essay. The complete "Enemy Within" document includes worked examples from data systems, organizations, therapy, and policy for each operator's reversal, plus the formal relationship between anti-transformations and the ±1/synthesis pole architecture.

Each is a deeper-layer upgrade. The MVP is sufficient for diagnosis, generation, and self-aware interpretation.

Development History

v1 — The Bare Elements (~200 words)

Five elements only: Lattice, Helix, Principle, Parse, Diagnostic.

Field test: ChatGPT walked through all nine operators sequentially, gave each equal airtime, and never caught itself performing operators. Competent classification. No diagnosis.

Failure mode: The AI treated the helix as a to-do list.

v2 — Analytical Posture (~400 words)

Added "HOW TO USE THIS" with five instructions: diagnose don't inventory, trace dependencies, weight your analysis, watch yourself, hold contradictions.

Field test: Diagnosed three specific blockages instead of walking all nine. Traced dependencies backward. Named its own SEN and SEG moves. Generatingd operator-native solutions (e.g., "Contradiction Budgets").

Failure mode: Self-watching was performative — labeled moves but didn't name costs. No DEF on its own output. Solutions didn't predict next-problems.

v3 — Cost Accounting and Self-SUP (~500 words)

Tightened "watch yourself" to require naming costs. Added "Turn DEF on your own output."

Field test: Every solution acquired operator-specific cost predictions. Output ended with explicit self-DEF: three named tensions, none prematurely resolved.

Failure mode: No forward prediction. No pattern-naming. No REC on framework.

v4 — Prediction, Pattern, and Framework Humility (~550 words)

Added "Predict forward," "Name the pattern," and "Turn REC on the framework."

Field test: Solutions generated predicted next-problems traced through helix. Diagnoses unified under coined pattern-name ("Floating Syncretism"). Framework limitations identified. Integrated solution stack with dependency ordering.

v5 — Translation Layer (~570 words)

Added "Translate on output." Operator names become AI's private coordinate system; output defaults to plain language.

Finding: Value is structural behavior, not vocabulary. "We're trying to merge everything without drawing boundaries first" lands better than "SYN fails because SEG is mushy."

v6 — Cross-Domain Bridge (~800 words)

Added "Push past the first-order solution" with cross-domain import instruction.

Field test (5 models): DeepSeek with DeepThink produced Ministry of Unsolicited Advice (an institution whose sole purpose is holding contradictions visible without resolving them) — a structural invention generated by asking "what kind of institution directly addresses SUP?" Cross-domain bridge worked. Reasoning chain: 12 seconds.

Finding: Reasoning models (chain-of-thought) trade depth for novelty. The "push past" instruction counteracts this by redirecting from domain-locked reasoning to operator-first reasoning. The ~800-word version outperforms a condensed ~600-word version on reasoning models because fuller phrasing reduces interpretive loops.

v7 — Three Faces + Operator Composition (~1,150 words)

Added four structural additions: (1) Composition — operators nest: pipeline and self-application. (2) Three Questions — terrain → operator → grain sequence. (3) Diagnostic seeds — desert cells as leverage points, next-operator-not-more-of-current rule. (4) Calibration — match analytical depth to problem complexity.

Rationale: Field tests showed (a) models diagnosing operators without locating terrain first, (b) operators used as bare labels without arguments, (c) no cross-operator compositions (models said "SEG then CON" as a sequence but never CON(SEG(x)) as a nested operation).

v8 — Two Registers + Nesting as Thesis (~1,100 words)

Rebuilt from scratch for naive AI onboarding. Tested iteratively against cold Claude instances using a climate intervention prompt.

Development sub-versions:

  • v8a (~1,650 words): Narrative rewrite. Led with the problem EO solves. Full nine-cell vocabulary for all three faces. Failure: Output read as essay about EO rather than analysis with EO. No notation deployed.

  • v8b (~960 words): Stripped exposition, imperative register. Added resolution bias warning. Failure: Parse and diagnostic taught as parallel tools — AI used the generative one and shelved the mechanical one.

  • v8c (~1,120 words): Embedded notation requirement inside Step 2. Full helix prediction chain enumerated. Failure: "Translate on output" instruction eating "use notation" instruction. AI chose plain language everywhere.

  • v8d (~1,100 words): Three structural changes. (1) Notation promoted to its own section before the diagnostic, with nesting as the payoff. Core claim: "If your diagnosis is all single operators, you're naming symptoms. The mechanism is in the nesting." (2) Two registers explicitly separated. (3) Step 2 includes nesting prompt.

Finding: Naive AIs reliably learn the three-face architecture and Ground/Figure bias from a single spec read. They do not reliably learn notation or helix tracing without (a) nesting presented as the analytical payoff rather than as formalism, and (b) explicit separation of reasoning and output registers.

v9 — The Helix-Structured Spec (~2,400 words)

Complete architectural redesign. The spec is no longer a reference document with protocol instructions. The spec is itself a helix traversal. Each section IS the instruction it teaches, performed on the reader's understanding.

Design thesis: If the cognitive protocol mirrors reality — if the way you use EO is structurally identical to the way transformation works — then separating the instruction set from the protocol is a design error. The medium should be the message. The spec should be a helix traversal, not a description of one.

Key structural changes over v8:

  1. The spec performs itself. Sections are NUL through REC. The reader undergoes the protocol by reading the document. The AI internalizes the protocol as a mode of cognition rather than as a checklist.

  2. NUL section demands real clearing. Not "step 1: read the site face" but "what framework are you currently using? Name it. Set it aside." A performed demand, not a described step. This primes the AI to perform a real NUL on its target problem rather than a ritual one.

  3. SEN includes a fertility test. "Your Ground distinction should be specific enough to change what the rest of the analysis produces. If you could swap it for a generic phrase and nothing downstream changes, it's not specific enough." This is the single highest-leverage quality gate in the spec (see Comparative Findings below).

  4. INS requires a populated Site Face table. Mandatory, not optional. Filling all nine cells forces the AI to discover where the problem actually lives rather than defaulting to the first plausible site.

  5. REC requires cascade prediction. "Follow at least two links. If your intervention is REC, what NULs does it force?" Prevents single-link predictions that stop before the interesting second-order effects.

  6. "One traversal, not a list" as an explicit rule. Directly prevents the most common failure mode of v7/v8: generating solutions in parallel and tagging them with operators afterward.

Development sequence:

  • v9a (~2,200 words): Initial helix-structured draft. Tested against cold Claude instance with climate prompt. Result: Produced output qualitatively superior to any v8 variant — deeper NUL, more specific SEN, solutions derived from diagnosis rather than listed in parallel. The AI treated the framework as a reasoning mode rather than a classification system. Failure: No fertility test on SEN, no mandatory Site table, no cascade prediction requirement. Output occasionally shallow at SEN and narrow at REC.

  • v9b (~2,400 words): Added four refinements based on comparative analysis of outputs across spec versions (see below): (1) SEN fertility test, (2) mandatory Site Face table at INS, (3) cascade prediction at REC, (4) "one traversal, not a list" rule. These target the specific quality gaps between good and excellent output.

Finding: The architectural shift from "reference + protocol" to "spec-as-helix" is the single largest quality improvement in the spec's history. It changes what the AI does with EO — from applying operators to problems (classification with extra steps) to reasoning through operators as a cognitive sequence (genuine diagnostic thinking). The mechanism: when the spec performs itself, the AI learns the protocol by undergoing it rather than by reading about it. This is the same principle the framework itself describes — INS (instantiation) teaches what SEN (distinction) alone cannot.

Comparative Findings

Tested across cold Claude instances using a standard climate intervention prompt. Four output types observed, corresponding to different spec architectures:

Type A — Instruction Set as Reference Manual

Produced by: The nine-instruction formal specification (signatures, preconditions, failure modes, composition rules) without the diagnostic protocol.

Characteristics: Lists 6–8 solutions in parallel. Each tagged with a full helix sequence and R3 resolution register. Identifies which instruction current approaches skip. Technically correct.

Strengths: Precise failure-mode identification ("CON before SEG produces noise"). Formal composition rules applied correctly. Explicit about what's missing from current systems.

Weaknesses: The helix sequences are labels, not reasoning. The AI generated solutions first and mapped them to operators second. Evidence: every solution runs nearly the full helix — real reasoning finds the 2–3 operators where the energy lives. No nesting. No backward trace. No site reading. No self-DEF. Uses the Act Face only; ignores Site and Resolution Faces.

Diagnostic: The instruction set is necessary but not sufficient. It gives the AI the formal vocabulary and dependency logic but doesn't teach how to think. The result is inventory, not diagnosis.

Type B — v8 Spec (Reference + Protocol)

Produced by: v8d — lattice, notation, four-step diagnostic, two registers, rules.

Characteristics: 6 solutions, each with four registers: Site, Diagnosis (nested notation), Intervention pipeline, Resolution grain. Backward trace and forward prediction per solution. Self-SUP section.

Strengths: Nesting. REC(SEN(emissions, property)) failing because SEN(emissions, liability) never stabilized — that's mechanism, not label. Full dependency traces: SYN(cap regimes) fails ← CON(registry, measurement) weak ← SEG(access, boundary) porous ← INS(credible permits) uneven. Self-SUP on the analysis itself including framework strain points.

Weaknesses: Solutions still presented in parallel rather than derived in sequence. Protocol is behind the curtain — you see excellent outputs but not the reasoning process that produced them. Site readings are present but brief (two-word labels, not populated tables).

Diagnostic: The protocol is working but the AI treats it as a checklist applied independently to each solution. The "diagnose, don't inventory" instruction reduces the inventory from nine operators to 2–3 per solution, but the solutions themselves are still inventoried.

Type C — v9a Spec (Helix-Structured, No Refinements)

Produced by: First helix-structured draft without fertility test, mandatory Site table, or cascade prediction.

Characteristics: One continuous helix traversal on the target problem. Each section is the next operator in the helix, performed live. Solutions emerge from the reasoning rather than being presented as outputs.

Strengths: The NUL is real — names specific frames being cleared and explains what they pre-fill. The SEN finds something the analyst didn't start with. The SEG names its exclusion cost. Nesting goes 2–3 levels deep. Solutions are derived from the diagnosis, not listed. Forward predictions are mechanical (derived from helix). The AI discovers things it didn't pre-plan.

Weaknesses: SEN occasionally produces correct but inert distinctions (true but not generative). Site reading compressed to a few named sites rather than a populated table. Forward prediction follows one link rather than tracing the cascade. Pedagogically transparent in a way that makes it a better teaching document than analytical document.

Diagnostic: The architectural shift to helix-structured spec is the key breakthrough. The AI treats the protocol as a mode of cognition. The remaining failures are about depth (how specific is the SEN? how far does the prediction trace?) rather than mode (is the AI classifying or diagnosing?).

Type D — v9b Spec (Helix-Structured + Refinements)

Produced by: Helix-structured spec with fertility test, mandatory Site table, cascade prediction, and "one traversal" rule.

Characteristics: One continuous helix traversal. Deep, specific NUL. Fertile SEN that changes everything downstream. Fully populated Site Face table. Solutions that are genuinely novel — strange enough to resist categorization, which is the point. Multi-link cascade predictions. Honest self-SUP including SUP-within-SUP (tension between the analysis's own Ground-bias and urgent Figure-work).

Specific quality markers over Type C:

  • SEN fertility. Type C: "fossil-fueled modernity as default background" — correct, abstract. Type D: "the felt disconnection between human metabolic activity and planetary metabolic activity" — specific, embodied, generates the entire downstream analysis. Everything flows from this distinction. The metabolic framing changes what CON means, what REC targets, and what solutions look like. The distinction is load-bearing.

  • Site reading. Type C: names two sites and moves on. Type D: populates the full 3×3 table, reads the Ground row, names three specific sites (Void, Field, Atmosphere), then makes the diagnostic move: "This is why policy keeps reaching for Figure-row interventions that miss the substrate."

  • Solution novelty. Type C: Planetary Sabbaths, Carbon Membrane Trade Zones, Contradiction Councils — structurally sound, recognizable policy proposals. Type D: atmospheric sensing as sonic/tactile/olfactory public infrastructure; energy as throughput gradient management; cities as metabolic organisms; institutions with dual accounting (economic and thermodynamic). These resist categorization because they emerge from a SEN that current framing doesn't have.

  • Cascade prediction. Type C: "If we implement SEG → CON pressure." One link. Type D: "If we restructure SEN(human, nature) → NUL pressure → most institutional categories dissolve. Economy/ecology, city/hinterland, production/waste — all downstream of the original SEN." Two links, with the second producing a genuinely unsettling insight the analyst didn't pre-plan.

  • Self-DEF. Type C: three tensions stated cleanly. Type D: same three tensions plus a DEF within the DEF — "we need rapid Figure-interventions AND long-arc Ground-restructuring, and neither can substitute for the other." The analysis holds a contradiction in its own relationship to urgency.

Diagnostic: The four refinements (fertility test, mandatory Site table, cascade prediction, one-traversal rule) target the specific quality gaps between C and D. They are all depth interventions — they don't change the architecture (which is already correct) but force the AI to go deeper at the three steps where depth matters most: SEN, INS (Site reading), and REC (prediction).

Design Principles

The spec follows the helix. v1–v3 = Existence (the framework exists in context). v4–v8 = Structure (the framework relates to itself). v9 = Significance (the framework performs itself). The development process instantiated the dependency ordering it describes.

The medium is the message. The v9 spec teaches by being a helix traversal, not by describing one. This is the single most important architectural decision. It changes what the AI does with EO — from classification to cognition.

Instructions over content. The structural elements (~540 words in v8, ~1,200 in v9) are necessary but not sufficient. The quality gates and thinking directives convert vocabulary into analytical competence.

Each instruction targets a specific failure. No instruction was added speculatively. Each was added because a field test produced a specific failure that the instruction corrects. This is the spec's own SEN→INS hygiene.

Depth over breadth. One helix traversal that finds the root produces better output than multiple parallel analyses that label the surface. The "one traversal, not a list" rule was added in v9 because every prior version's most common failure mode was parallel inventory rather than sequential diagnosis.

Fertile over correct. The SEN fertility test is the highest-leverage quality gate. The difference between good and excellent output lives almost entirely in the quality of the initial Ground distinction. A correct SEN produces true labels. A fertile SEN produces a reading that generates novel insights at every subsequent step. The spec must teach the AI to aim for fertility.

Teach structure, not vocabulary. The AI will generate its own metaphors and terminology. The spec teaches the lattice and lets the AI find language native to each problem.

Usage Notes

Model-agnostic, capability-dependent. Any instruction-following model will attempt the right shape. The harder instructions (genuine dependency tracing, specific forward prediction, real self-DEF) require a capability threshold. Small models produce the skeleton. Larger models produce the reasoning.

Reasoning modes trade depth for novelty. Models with extended reasoning (chain-of-thought, "think" modes) produce more methodical, thorough, and conventional output. Each reasoning step is a small safe move; enough of them converge toward well-known solutions. The "push past" instruction and the fertility test counteract this.

The longer version outperforms the shorter one on reasoning models. v9's ~2,400 words give chain-of-thought models more to grab onto per instruction, reducing interpretive loops. The v8 ~1,100-word version still works well on non-reasoning models.

Combinable. The MVP is a reasoning framework, not a persona. It combines with other system prompts, style instructions, or domain-specific context.

Upgradeable. The full 27-cell capacity ground is the first upgrade for fine-grained work. The instruction set (nine formally specified operations with signatures, preconditions, and failure-mode catalog) is the upgrade for implementation and formal verification. Entity types for classification. Experience Organ rules for system design.

Self-testing. Ask the AI to analyze a wicked problem. Signs of competence:

  • Locates the terrain before reaching for an operator (populates Site Face)

  • Performs a real NUL (names specific frames being cleared, not ritual clearing)

  • Produces a fertile SEN (Ground distinction that changes everything downstream)

  • Names operators with their arguments — target and operand, not bare labels

  • Nests to at least two levels (mechanism, not symptoms)

  • Identifies 2–3 operators where energy lives (not all nine)

  • Traces dependencies backward through the helix with filled arguments

  • Names costs of its own moves

  • Predicts forward consequences with at least two cascade links

  • Holds tensions in its own output without resolving them

  • Questions the framework's limits

Signs of failure:

  • Walks all nine operators sequentially

  • Gives equal airtime to all operators

  • Uses bare operator labels ("SEG failure") without specifying target and operand

  • Doesn't name its own analytical moves

  • Treats all problems as entity-level (Figure) when they live on Ground

  • Resolves contradictions into clean narratives

  • Proposes well-known solutions with operator labels attached

  • Prescribes more of the operator a system is already stuck in

  • Generatings solutions in parallel rather than deriving them from a single traversal

  • SEN produces a correct but generic Ground that doesn't change downstream analysis

  • Predictions follow one link without tracing the cascade
